summary: Joel briefly mentions MP3pro, a new audio format that achieves half the file size of MP3 with better quality while maintaining backward compatibility with existing MP3 players. He notes it eliminates Windows Media's technical advantage. He dismisses a Gartner analyst's concern about record label adoption as irrelevant. The post ends with an unrelated note about salvaging a vintage Bang & Olufsen stereo receiver.
key_points: MP3pro offered 50% size reduction over MP3 with higher quality and backward compatibility
The format neutralized Windows Media's technical advantages over MP3
Joel dismisses the importance of major record label buy-in for audio format success, siding with user adoption over industry gatekeepers
The post reflects Joel's characteristic interest in consumer technology and contrarian takes on industry dynamics
money_quotes: I've been playing with MP3pro, a new file format that is exactly half the size of MP3s, with higher sound quality.
The even neater part is that you can play MP3pro's with your old MP3 player.
And it eliminates Windows Media's technical advantage.
Hello? Who cares what record companies do?
Remember RCA? I thought they were long gone.
key_insight: Backward compatibility and technical merit matter more than industry gatekeeper approval when it comes to technology adoption.
